titleEn: Mix and match to build your dream drone trailer
titleZh: 混搭定制：打造你的梦想植保无人机拖车
summaryZh: 本文报道了印第安纳州植保无人机协会教育日上展示的多种定制无人机拖车方案。从业者分享了从双层平板到封闭式拖车、淡水箱、混合罐、双泵系统及空调指挥中心等配置经验，强调拖车随作业需求不断进化，可大幅提升作业效率和清洁便利性。
translationZh:
植保无人机拖车就像它们所承载的无人机一样多样化。你需要一辆双层平板拖车还是一辆小型封闭式拖车？想要一个淡水箱吗？在飞行无人机时，放几把舒适的椅子坐下来怎么样？

从头开始建造一辆植保无人机拖车会带来这些问题以及更多。一次性做出所有这些决定可能令人望而生畏，但大多数操作员发现，他们的拖车会随着他们一起进化，尤其是当它们是从零开始建造的时候。

“你可以完全按照你想要的方式拥有它，”印第安纳州北弗农的LM农业服务公司老板兰斯·马斯基诺说。他的拖车是在印第安纳州植保无人机协会教育日上展示的拖车之一。他和其他几位操作员分享了他们在拖车上发现的有用组件。

**不断进化的项目**

印第安纳州福勒的阿尔伯森无人机服务公司老板凯尔·阿尔伯森现在拥有他的第二辆植保无人机拖车，他向与会者展示了他最新升级的植保无人机拖车，以及他最初使用的那辆较小的封闭式拖车。

阿尔伯森的新拖车现已进入第二个使用季，可容纳1000加仑淡水、一个150加仑混合罐、两个泵、一个用于存放电池和充电器的储物箱，以及一个带空调的指挥中心。这比他第一辆可容纳500加仑水的拖车更进了一步，那辆拖车仍在他小规模作业时使用。

在他的新拖车上配备两个泵和淡水，可以实现更顺畅的操作和更高的效率。

“我们发现，拥有淡水进行清理更容易、更好，”阿尔伯森说。“这样，我们就可以使用软管卷盘。这是干净的水；你可以洗手、清洗无人机以及所有其他东西。而且没有污染。”

定制第二辆植保无人机拖车让阿尔伯森有机会改进和调整组件，以更好地满足他的需求。这也是其他操作员（如马斯基诺）在自行建造无人机拖车的灵活性方面所欣赏的一点。

点击幻灯片，仔细查看可能最适合你需求的植保无人机拖车组件。
featuredReason: 本文聚焦植保无人机拖车的定制化趋势，提供了从业者从第一代到第二代拖车的真实升级案例，涵盖淡水系统、双泵配置、空调指挥中心等关键组件，对智慧畜牧和精准农业从业者具有实操参考价值，尤其适合大田作物场景下的无人机作业优化。
contentFull:
Farming Equipment

Technology

Farm Business Planning

News

Mix and match to build your dream drone trailer

Slideshow: Glean ideas from these custom spray drone trailers on display at the Indiana Spray Drone Association Education Day.

Allison Lynch,Staff Writer,Indiana Prairie FarmerJuly 16, 2026

photo_library11 Slides

play_arrowStart slideshow

Photos by Allison Lynch

Spray drone trailers are as diverse as the drones that occupy them. Do you need a double-decker flatbed or a small, enclosed trailer? Would you like a freshwater tank? How about comfy chairs for taking a seat while flying the drone?

Building a spray drone trailer from scratch brings these questions and more to light. It can be daunting to make these decisions all at once, but most operators find that their trailers evolve with them, especially when they are built from scratch.

“You can have it exactly how you want it,” said Lance Maschino, owner of LM Ag Services in North Vernon, Ind. His trailer was one on display at the Indiana Spray Drone Association Education Day. He and several other operators shared the components they’ve found useful on their trailers.

Evolving projects

Now on his second spray drone trailer, Kyle Albertson, owner of Albertson Drone Service in Fowler, Ind., gave attendees a look at not only his most recent upgraded spray drone trailer but also the smaller enclosed trailer he started with.

Related:‘CurseBuster’ tillage tool changes the soil health game

Albertson’s new trailer, now in its second season, houses 1,000 gallons of freshwater, a 150-gallon mixing tank, two pumps, a storage box for batteries and chargers, and an air-conditioned command center. It’s a step up from his first trailer, which holds 500 gallons of water and is still in his rotation for small jobs.

Having two pumps and freshwater on his new trailer allows for smoother operation and greater efficiency.

“We found it was easier and nicer for cleanout that we can have fresh water,” Albertson said. “That way, we can use the hose reel. It’s clean water; you can wash your hands, wash the drones and everything else. And there’s no contamination.”

Customizing a second spray drone trailer gave Albertson the chance to improve and tweak components to better fit his needs. This is something other operators, such as Maschino, appreciate when it comes to the flexibility of building your own drone trailer.

Click through the slideshow to get a closer look at the spray drone trailer components that may best fit your needs.

About the Author

Allison Lynch

Staff Writer, Indiana Prairie Farmer

Allison Lynch, formerly Allison Lund, is a staff writer for Indiana Prairie Farmer. She graduated from Purdue University with a major in agricultural communications and a minor in crop science. She served as president of Purdue’s Agricultural Communicators of Tomorrow chapter. In 2022, she received the American FFA Degree.

Lynch grew up as the oldest of four children on a cash grain farm in south-central Wisconsin, where the primary crops were corn, soybeans, wheat and alfalfa. Her family also raised chewing tobacco and Hereford cattle. She spent most of her time helping with the tobacco crop in the summer and raising Boer goats for FFA projects. She now lives near Winamac, Ind, where her husband farms with his family.
